title: Increment And Decrement A Number
goal: Use `<C-a>` to bump the number on this line from 41 to 42.
explanation: <C-a> increments the number under (or right of) the cursor; <C-x> decrements. Counts compose: 5<C-a> adds five.
rule: <C-a>/<C-x> turn arithmetic into a single keystroke.

check.success_text: You bumped a number with <C-a> instead of retyping it.

title: Decrement Via <C-x>
goal: Use `<C-x>` to drop the number from 100 to 99.
explanation: <C-x> is the mirror of <C-a>. Like all counting motions, it accepts a count: 10<C-x> subtracts ten.
rule: <C-x> mirrors <C-a>; both honor counts.

check.success_text: You decremented a number with <C-x>.

title: Join Lines With J
goal: Use `J` to merge `hello` and `world` onto one line, separated by a space.
explanation: J joins the next line to the current one with a single space. gJ does the same without the separator.
hints: Press J on the first line.
rule: J = join with space; gJ = join raw.

check.success_text: You joined two lines with a single keystroke.

title: Toggle Case
goal: Place the cursor on `h` and press `~` to flip it to `H`.
explanation: ~ toggles the case of the character under the cursor and advances. g~iw toggles a whole word.
hints: Press ~ once on the lowercase h.
rule: ~ for one char, g~iw for a word, g~~ for a line.

check.success_text: You flipped a character's case with ~.

title: Uppercase A Word
goal: Use `gUiw` to uppercase `hello` in place.
explanation: gU is the uppercase operator. With iw, it transforms the inner word; with iw replaced by ip, it would transform a paragraph.
rule: Case operators are full operators; combine with any text-object.

check.success_text: You uppercased an inner word in one operator + text-object.

title: ROT13 The Line
goal: Use `g??` to rot13 the current line.
explanation: g? is the rot13 operator. The duplicate form g?? operates on the current line. Mostly a curiosity, occasionally handy.
rule: Every Vim user should know it exists. Run g?? once for the trivia.

check.success_text: You rot13'd a line. Your team will think you're a wizard.

title: Resume Insert With gi
goal: Press `gi` to drop straight back into Insert mode at the position you last left it.
explanation: gi remembers where Insert mode last ended (the auto-mark '^) and resumes there. Saves the i / a dance after a quick navigation aside.
hints: You start in Normal at row 1.
Press gi — Vim drops back into Insert mode at the position where Insert last ended.
rule: gi is `'^` + i in one keystroke.

check.success_text: gi dropped you back into Insert mode in one keystroke.

title: Register An Autocommand
goal: Run `:autocmd BufWritePre * %s/\s\+$//e` so trailing whitespace gets stripped on save.
explanation: :autocmd Event Pattern Cmd binds Cmd to fire on Event for files matching Pattern. The trainer stores it; real Vim wires the event system.
hints: Run the autocmd line and press Enter.
rule: autocmd is the bridge between events and edits; learn the syntax even if your config never grows beyond a handful.

check.success_text: You registered an autocommand. Save events would now strip trailing whitespace.

title: Use An Augroup
goal: Wrap a `BufRead *.go` autocmd in an augroup with `:augroup gocfg` ... `:augroup END`.
explanation: augroup names a logical bucket so you can clear it cleanly with `:autocmd!` inside the same group. It's how plugin authors avoid stacking duplicate autocmds across reloads.
canonical_solutions: :augroup gocfg Enter :autocmd BufRead *.go echo "go!" Enter :augroup END Enter
rule: Always group your autocmds; you'll thank yourself when reloading config.

check.success_text: You scoped an autocmd inside a named augroup.

title: Save A Session
goal: Use `:mksession ~/work.vim` to capture the layout.
explanation: :mksession serializes the current windows / buffers / options to a file you can re-source later with :source.
rule: Sessions are layout snapshots; they make multi-window setups disposable.

check.success_text: You saved a session — you can :source it later to recover the layout.

title: Save A View
goal: Use `:mkview` to capture per-file fold + cursor state.
explanation: :mkview persists this file's view (folds, cursor) so :loadview restores it. Pair with autocmds (BufWritePost / BufRead) to make it automatic.
rule: mkview is per-file; mksession is for the whole layout.

check.success_text: You saved a view. :loadview will bring back folds and cursor next time.

title: Customize The Statusline
goal: Set `:set statusline=%f` so the bottom bar shows the current filename.
explanation: statusline is a format string. %f is the file path; %m is the modified flag; %y is filetype; %l/%L is line/total. Plugins like lualine ship richer renderers.
rule: The statusline is a format string; keep it readable, not a metric soup.

check.success_text: Your statusline format is set.

title: Conceal Markup
goal: Set `:set conceallevel=2` so concealed text (Markdown, LaTeX) renders.
explanation: conceallevel controls how the editor renders 'conceal' regions defined by syntax files. 0 disables; 2 shows replacement char if defined; 3 hides entirely.
rule: Markdown and LaTeX users live with conceallevel=2 or 3.

check.success_text: Conceal is on; syntax-defined hidden regions will render compactly.

title: Place A Sign
goal: Run `:sign place name=Bookmark line=2 buffer=1` to drop a sign.
explanation: Signs are gutter glyphs that linters, debuggers, and version-control plugins use to mark lines. Define them with :sign define, then place them with :sign place.
hints: Run the :sign place command and Enter.
rule: Signs are how every gutter plugin you've ever used surfaces information.

check.success_text: You placed a sign — gutter plugins use this exact API.

title: Open The Cmdline History
goal: Run `:set number`, then press `q:` to open your cmdline history as an editable buffer.
explanation: q: opens command history as a buffer you can scroll, edit, and re-execute. q/ does the same for search history. Underused; massively powerful.
rule: q: and q/ turn ephemeral history into editable text.

check.success_text: You opened the cmdline-history buffer.

title: Search Help With :helpgrep
goal: Run `:helpgrep autocmd` to fill quickfix with help-tag matches.
explanation: :helpgrep PATTERN scans the help tags and dumps results into quickfix — your first stop when `:help foo` lands on the wrong page.
rule: :helpgrep + :cnext beats clicking through ten :help pages.

check.success_text: You searched help via :helpgrep — quickfix has the hits.

title: Jump To A Tag With <C-]>
goal: Move onto `deploy` and press `<C-]>` to jump to its definition.
explanation: <C-]> follows the tag under the cursor — in real Vim, that means a tags-file lookup. The trainer reuses the gd heuristic so you can practice the keystroke.
rule: <C-]> follows; <C-T> goes back. The two keystrokes are 90% of tag navigation.

check.success_text: You jumped via tag — and <C-T> would pop you back.

title: Toggle Treesitter Playground
goal: Run `:TSPlaygroundToggle` to open the syntax tree explorer.
explanation: Treesitter parses code into a syntax tree the editor can highlight, fold, and select against. The playground shows the tree live — invaluable when authoring queries.
rule: When highlighting feels off, the playground is the first place to look.

check.success_text: You toggled the Treesitter playground.

title: Start A Debug Session
goal: Run `:DapToggleBreakpoint` then `:DapContinue` to drive a debug adapter.
explanation: nvim-dap exposes Debug Adapter Protocol clients via these `:Dap*` commands. The trainer doesn't ship a real DAP backend — these are vocabulary lessons.
rule: Breakpoint + continue is the loop you'll spend the most time in.

check.success_text: You drove the DAP loop — breakpoint + continue.

title: Browse Available Snippets
goal: Run `:LuaSnipListAvailable` to see the snippets registered for the current filetype.
explanation: LuaSnip is the most popular snippet engine for Neovim. Its `:LuaSnipListAvailable` command lists what would expand at the cursor.
rule: When `<Tab>` won't expand, list available — your filetype probably has no snippets registered.

check.success_text: You queried the snippet engine — useful when expansions feel broken.
